serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving of cooked rice vermicelli in Singapore is approximately 200 g, equivalent to one plate as commonly served at hawker centres and food courts.

nutrient_source.title: Rice vermicelli, cooked — HPB Singapore Foodo / USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (Rice noodles, cooked, NDB 20113)
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values for cooked rice vermicelli sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for rice noodles, cooked (NDB 20113 / FDC ID 169726), cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Foodo data for bee hoon (cooked). Both sources are consistent. Per-100 g values reflect the cooked state as consumed.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — rice noodles, dried, boiled (GI 61); Atkinson FS et al. Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Rice vermicelli (mihun / bee hoon) is listed in the University of Sydney GI Database and the Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables. The GI of cooked rice noodles / rice vermicelli ranges from 53 to 61 across studies; a representative value of 58 is used here, consistent with the mean reported for rice noodles boiled. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (58 × 49.6) / 100 ≈ 28.8, rounded to 28.7.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content in plain cooked rice vermicelli
hypertension.guidance: Plain cooked rice vermicelli contains approximately 33 mg sodium per 100 g, making it a low-sodium staple; however, sodium load increases substantially when served with sauces, broths, or seasoned stir-fry, so individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sauce preparations and monitor condiment use.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (GI 58) and high carbohydrate density
type2_diabetes.guidance: Rice vermicelli has a medium GI of approximately 58 and delivers a glycemic load of around 28–29 per standard 200 g serving, which can cause a moderate postprandial glucose rise; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size, pair with high-fibre vegetables and lean protein to blunt the glycemic response, and prefer souped preparations over oil-heavy stir-fry.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low fat and saturated fat content in plain cooked form
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Plain cooked rice vermicelli is very low in total fat (0.2 g/100 g) and saturated fat (0.1 g/100 g), posing minimal direct cardiovascular risk from the noodle itself; cardiovascular concern arises primarily from cooking methods that add oil, lard, or high-sodium sauces, so steamed or lightly souped preparations are preferred for individuals managing cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Rice vermicelli, known in Singapore as bee hoon or mihun (米粉, Mǐfěn), is a thin, translucent noodle made from rice flour and water. It is one of the most widely consumed staple noodles across Chinese, Singaporean, Malaysian, and broader Southeast Asian cuisines. The noodles are typically sold dried and must be soaked in water before cooking; they are then stir-fried, boiled in soup, or served in broth-based dishes. Common Singaporean preparations include fried bee hoon (stir-fried with vegetables, egg, and soy sauce), bee hoon soup (served in a light broth with fish balls or pork slices), and laksa bee hoon (in spiced coconut milk broth). The texture is soft and slightly chewy when cooked, with a mild, neutral flavour that readily absorbs surrounding sauces and seasonings. Nutritionally, cooked rice vermicelli is predominantly carbohydrate (approximately 24.8 g/100 g), low in protein (1.8 g/100 g), and very low in fat (0.2 g/100 g), with an energy density of approximately 109 kcal per 100 g. It is naturally gluten-free and suitable for halal and vegan diets. The glycemic index is approximately 58 (medium), and a standard 200 g serving delivers a glycemic load of approximately 28–29, which is clinically relevant for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. Sodium content is low in the plain cooked form but increases significantly with sauces and seasonings. Rice vermicelli is a culturally important food in Singapore hawker culture and is commonly served at breakfast, lunch, and dinner.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (rice noodles, cooked, FDC ID 169726), cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Foodo data for bee hoon. GI value sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables. Both are approved primary sources. The food identity (rice vermicelli / bee hoon / mihun) is unambiguous and well-documented.
notes: trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; rice noodles contain no trans fats by composition. sugar_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy; plain cooked rice vermicelli contains negligible free sugars. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a plant-based grain product containing no animal-derived ingredients. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy; rice noodles contain no vitamin C. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a plant-based product. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a plant-based grain product. vitamin_e_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy for cooked rice noodles. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are each 0.1 g per USDA SR Legacy. Sodium value of 33 mg/100 g reflects plain cooked rice vermicelli without added sauces or seasonings; actual sodium in restaurant or hawker preparations will be substantially higher. GI of 58 is a representative mean for rice noodles boiled, drawn from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008; individual GI values in the literature range from 53 to 61. GL per serving = (58 × 49.6 g carbohydrate in 200 g serving) / 100 = 28.8, reported as 28.7. The food is served at Hai Kah Lang (Funan Mall) in a hot pot context, which may involve additional ingredients and sauces not reflected in the plain cooked nutrient values. Ontology codes: FoodOn FOODON_03302669 (rice noodle) is an exact match. FoodEx2 code A00LC (rice noodles) is an exact match per EFSA FoodEx2 classification. SNOMED CT 226723007 (Rice noodles) is an exact match.
